pre_requisites: Students should have basic engineering drawing skills (reading simple 2D orthographic views
with basic dimensions/symbols), elementary workshop exposure (tolerances, surface finish,
safety norms), comfort with simple arithmetic and units, and general computer literacy
(files/folders, mouse navigation, basic 3D‑like interfaces) so this course can focus on CAD,
welding, and parametric modeling.
expected_outcomes: Students will be able to explain and safely apply basic welding processes, create simple
parametric 3D parts and small assemblies in a modern CAD tool, and redesign components for
3D printing by applying core design‑for‑additive principles and understanding their impact on
quality, time, and cost.

topics.title: Welding processes (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW) – 8 h
Joint design, symbols, quality, safety – 6 h
Simple weld procedure + shop demo/videos – 4 h
2D to 3D thinking, constraints, parametric modeling – 8 h
Simple part + small assembly modeling in a chosen CAD (e.g., SolidWorks intro) – 8 h
3D printing processes, materials, limitations – 6 h
Basic design rules for AM (overhangs, support, wall thickness) – 4 h
Mini exercise: redesign a simple bracket for printing – 4 h

pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable with basic 3D CAD (extrude/revolve/cut/fillet and parametric
modeling in SolidWorks or similar), simple mechanical design concepts and GD&T notions,
foundational mechanics and math (statics, stress/strain, units, basic plot interpretation), and
general Windows/software literacy (installing, managing files, iterating on models and handling
tool warnings) so this course can focus on applying CAD and simulation tools for design and
analysis.
expected_outcomes: Students will be able to create parametric parts, assemblies, and detailed drawings in SolidWorks
(including a small integrated design mini‑project with basic GD&T and simple automation via
macros) and set up, run, and interpret basic static structural simulations in Ansys Workbench,
using stress/displacement results and simple parameter studies to drive informed design
improvements.

topics.title: Sketches, features, parts – 10 h
Assemblies, mates, exploded views – 8 h
2D drawings, basic GD&T symbols – 6 h
Small design mini‑project – 4 h

details.title: Include a short demonstration of macro recording / automation (within the mini‑project) to expose students to CAD scripting concepts.

topics.title: Geometry import/cleanup, meshing – 6 h
Static structural analysis steps – 8 h
Interpreting results, simple design changes – 6 h

details.title: Include a simple parametric study (e.g., 2–3 thickness or load variants) to illustrate the idea of repeated/batch runs and prepare students for scripted/automated analysis in III‑I.

pre_requisites: Students should have a strong command of SolidWorks (parts, assemblies, drawings, basic
GD&T, from the “SolidWorks and Ansys Foundations” level), prior Ansys Workbench
experience for static structural analysis, foundational manufacturing knowledge
(machining/welding/casting, tolerances and cost), and solid math/mechanics plus basic technical
report‑writing skills, so this course can focus on more advanced CAD/CAE applications and
engineering decision‑making.
expected_outcomes: Students will be able to design and document configurable, manufacturable parts and assemblies
in SolidWorks (including weldments, sheet‑metal, design tables, BOMs, and exploded views), set
up and interpret modal/thermal analyses and parameter sweeps in Ansys to drive
simulation‑based design decisions, and apply DFM/DFA and cost‑awareness principles to choose
suitable processes and simplify designs for lower cost and higher reliability.

topics.title: Configurations, design tables – 6 h
Top‑down assemblies, weldments/sheet metal – 10 h
BOMs, exploded views, drawing packs – 6 h
Modal / simple thermal analysis – 8 h
Design iteration based on results – 6 h
Basic report creation – 4 h

details.text: Embed a small data‑driven mini‑exercise: students run a short parameter sweep (mesh density, thickness, load or material) and include graphs/tables plus a 1‑page interpretation in the report.

topics.title: Machining vs welding vs casting – 4 h
Tolerancing and cost drivers – 4 h

pre_requisites: Students should have a strong working knowledge of advanced SolidWorks and Ansys (up
through III‑I level: configurations, top‑down assemblies, weldments/sheet metal,
static/modal/thermal FEA with reporting), sound DFM/DFA and manufacturing awareness, and
solid technical communication skills (clear drawings, concise reports, presentations) along with
basic digital/AI literacy, so this capstone course can focus on higher‑level CAD/CAE design
decisions and engineering communication.
expected_outcomes: Students will be able to convert functional requirements into manufacturable CAD/CAE designs
(full assemblies with drawings, BOMs, and simulation‑driven iterations), use basic automation
(macros/parameter studies) to reduce manual effort, and participate in industry‑style reviews with
a documented portfolio that transparently records and critically evaluates any AI‑assisted
ideation or checks used in their capstone project.

topics.title: Concept + requirements – 4 h
CAD modeling & assemblies – 12 h
FEA runs & iterations – 10 h
Manufacturing drawings & documentation – 6 h
Design reviews, critique sessions – 8 h

details.title: Where possible, include reviews by external/industry experts and/or live problem statements from partner companies.

topics.title: Basic portfolio creation (reports, screenshots, videos) – 4 h
AI‑assisted ideation / checks (with validation) – 4 h

details.title: Structured AI use: 2 h on idea generation and concept refinement, 2 h on AI‑assisted checking of loads, constraints, failure modes, or documentation, with explicit human validation steps.

semesters.description: Industrial Engineering & OR for Manufacturing – 60 h
pre_requisites: Prior exposure to manufacturing processes (machining, welding, casting), basic plant
layout ideas, and DFM/DFA from III‑I.
Working knowledge of CAD/CAE workflows (SolidWorks and Ansys up to III‑II),
including how designs translate to fixtures, tooling, and operations.
Comfort with basic math (simultaneous equations, inequalities, graphs) to support linear
programming and simple quantitative models.
expected_outcomes: Explain and apply key Industrial Engineering concepts such as PPC, work study, and
waste elimination to real or simulated manufacturing systems.
Formulate and solve basic optimization problems (linear programming and transportation
models) to support decisions in production, logistics, and layout.
Conduct a simplified method and time study on a selected operation, identify
non‑value‑added activities, and recommend changes to reduce cycle time and fatigue.
Relate IE & OR decisions to design/CAE choices (fixtures, line balancing, buffer sizing)
and communicate their impact on efficiency, cost, and quality.

modules.module_title: Industrial Engineering Overview & Role in Design/Manufacturing

topics.title: Evolution and scope of Industrial Engineering: productivity, ergonomics, cost, quality.
Interface with Design, CAE, and Manufacturing Engineering (how design affects methods, times, and layouts).
Introduction to Operations Research as a decision‑making toolkit in manufacturing.
PPC functions: forecasting link, planning, routing, scheduling, loading, dispatching, follow‑up, and feedback.
Material Requirement Planning (MRP): BOM, dependent demand, lead times, safety stock, and planned orders.
Simple scheduling and sequencing: Gantt idea, job sequencing rules and their effect on waiting time and utilization.
Mini‑exercise: Create a simple MRP table for a small assembly based on a CAD BOM.
Objectives and steps of method study: select, record, examine, develop, define, install, maintain.
Recording techniques: operation process chart, flow process chart, two‑hand process chart, and basic flow diagrams.
Application to manufacturing cells: identifying avoidable movements, transport, and delays.
Lab‑style activity: Map the method for a small assembly or lab process and propose an improved method.

modules.module_title: Work Study: Time Study and Time–Motion Analysis

topics.title: Purpose and procedure of time study: selecting the job, breaking into elements, timing, performance rating, allowances, and standard time.
Time and motion study together: analyzing motions to simplify work, reduce fatigue, and improve ergonomics.
Using time study data for line balancing, manpower planning, and setting realistic production targets.
Lab‑style activity: Conduct a basic time study for a repetitive task (manual or simulated video) and compute standard time.

modules.module_title: Optimization Tools: Linear Programming & Transportation Models

topics.title: Basics of linear programming (LP): decision variables, objective function, constraints, feasible region.
Simple LP problems in manufacturing: product mix selection, capacity allocation, and blending examples (graphical method for 2‑variable problems).
Transportation model: structure, cost matrix, feasibility, and interpretation in production and distribution networks.
Exercises: Formulate and solve (manually or with a simple solver) 1–2 LP and 1 transportation problem relevant to manufacturing/logistics.

modules.module_title: Simulation & Basic Queueing Ideas in Manufacturing

topics.title: Concept of discrete‑event simulation: events, entities, queues, and servers in a production context.
Typical applications: evaluating layouts, buffer sizes, and scheduling rules without disturbing the real plant.
Simple queueing ideas: arrival and service concepts, intuitive impact of utilization on waiting time.
Demonstration: Walkthrough of a simple manufacturing line simulation (spreadsheet or educational tool) and discussion.

modules.module_title: Waste Elimination, Lean Thinking, and Cycle Time Reduction

topics.title: Types of waste in manufacturing (overproduction, waiting, transport, extra processing, inventory, motion, defects) and mapping NVA vs VA activities.
Using work study and PPC data to target waste: examples of layout change, method improvement, and batch size optimization.
Case snippets: Published examples where cycle time reduction led to higher throughput and better quality.
Mini‑exercise: Identify NVA activities from a time‑study log and propose a new sequence to cut cycle time.
Problem framing: Choose a small product or sub‑assembly and define production requirements and constraints.
Apply PPC + work study + optimization:

details.title: Carry out a basic method/time study (real or video),
Suggest at least one LP or transportation‑type model relevant to the case.

topics.title: Deliverables: Short report (3–5 pages) and a brief presentation highlighting impact on cycle time, cost, and quality, linking back to CAD/CAE design choices. Company Specific Practice Tests – 12 h
Domain‑focused tests on mechanics, strength of materials, thermodynamics, and manufacturing fundamentals – 6 h
Company‑oriented caselets in CAD/CAE/manufacturing and IE (simple PPC, work study, or optimization scenarios) – 6 h Soft Skills – 18 h
Resume building and LinkedIn optimization – 4 h
Group discussions and technical presentations (with focus on explaining IE/CAE projects clearly) – 6 h
Mock interviews (technical + HR rounds, including questions on IE, PPC, work study, and projects) – 8 h
